title: Pharmacodynamics: Foundation
summary: الفارماكوديناميكا تدرس تأثير الدواء على الجسم، وتشمل فهم أهداف الدواء كالمستقبلات والإنزيمات وقنوات الأيونات. الأدوية قد تكون ناهضة (Agonist) أو مضادة (Antagonist) بحسب قدرتها على إحداث استجابة. تُقاس فاعلية الدواء بالـ Emax وقوته بالـ EC50، أما مؤشر السلامة فيُعبَّر عنه بالـ Therapeutic Index. يُعدّ التضاد الدوائي والتآزر وظاهرة التحمل من المفاهيم الأساسية في هذا الموضوع.

key_drugs.name: Adrenaline (Epinephrine)
key_drugs.note: مثال على ناهض كامل يرتبط بالمستقبل وينتج أقصى استجابة

key_drugs.name: Atropine
key_drugs.note: مضاد تنافسي للأستيل كولين، كفاءته صفر (Zero efficacy)

key_drugs.name: Neostigmine
key_drugs.note: يثبط إنزيم Cholinesterase بالتنافس مع Acetylcholine

key_drugs.name: Aspirin
key_drugs.note: يثبط Cyclooxygenase بصورة لا رجعة فيها

key_drugs.name: Histamine
key_drugs.note: مثال على التضاد الفسيولوجي مع Adrenaline في ضغط الدم

mnemonics.title: أهداف الدواء: RICE
mnemonics.body:
R = Receptor
I = Ion channel
C = Carrier molecule (Transporter)
E = Enzyme
كل دواء لازم يوصل لـ RICE عشان يشتغل

mnemonics.title: الناهض والمضاد: عريس وحارس
mnemonics.body: تخيل المستقبل بيت العروس. الـ Agonist هو العريس: عنده شوق للبيت (Affinity) وعنده قدرة يدخل ويغير الجو (Efficacy). الـ Antagonist هو الحارس: يقف عند الباب ويمنع غيره من الدخول، لكنه ما يغير شي داخل البيت (Zero Efficacy).

mnemonics.title: منحنى الجرعة-الاستجابة: جبل الدواء
mnemonics.body: تخيل جبلاً: ارتفاع الجبل = Emax (الفاعلية). الدواء الذي يصل الذروة بكمية قليلة = قوي (High Potency / Low EC50). دوائان يصلان نفس الذروة = متساويا الفاعلية رغم اختلاف القوة.

mnemonics.title: أنواع التضاد: PCPN
mnemonics.body:
P = Physiological (Functional) antagonism
C = Chemical antagonism
P = Pharmacokinetic antagonism
N = Non-competitive pharmacological antagonism
(+ Competitive كنوع فرعي)

mnemonics.title: التحمل والتاكيفيلاكسيس
mnemonics.body: تصور رجل يشرب قهوة كل يوم: في البداية كوب واحد يصحيه (Tolerance ببطء). لكن هناك من يشرب فجأة خمسة أكواب ويحس بالتعود في دقائق - هذا هو الـ Tachyphylaxis: تحمل سريع وحاد.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics?
flashcards.back: Pharmacokinetics: what the body does to the drug (ADME). Pharmacodynamics: what the drug does to the body (effect and response).

flashcards.front: What are the four drug targets?
flashcards.back: Receptors, Enzymes, Ion channels, Carrier molecules (RICE)

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Affinity?
flashcards.back: The tendency of a drug to bind to a receptor. A ligand is any molecule that has affinity for a specific receptor.

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Efficacy?
flashcards.back: The ability of a drug to produce the desired therapeutic effect (Emax). A full agonist has the highest efficacy.
A drug with full affinity and full efficacy — it binds to the receptor and produces the maximum possible response. Example: Adrenaline.
A drug that binds to the receptor (has affinity) but Efficacy = Zero. It produces no response but blocks other drugs. Example: Atropine.
It has affinity but partial (incomplete) efficacy. Alone it acts as an agonist; in the presence of a full agonist it acts as an antagonist.
The dose or concentration required to produce 50% of the maximum response. It is used to measure Potency — the lower the EC50, the greater the potency.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between Potency and Efficacy?
flashcards.back: They are independent. A drug may have high efficacy but low potency, and vice versa.
TI = TD50 / EC50. It measures the safety margin of a drug. The higher the TI, the safer the drug.
The range of doses that produces the desired therapeutic effect without causing unwanted toxic effects.
Two drugs act on different receptors and produce opposing effects. Example: Histamine lowers blood pressure; Adrenaline raises it.
A direct chemical reaction between two drugs that results in loss of activity. The drug is inactivated by chemical binding.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Competitive and Non-competitive antagonism?
flashcards.back: Competitive: the antagonist competes with the agonist at the same binding site — can be overcome by increasing the dose. Non-competitive: binds to a different site — cannot be overcome by increasing the dose (reduces Emax).

flashcards.front: What is Additive vs Supraadditive (Potentiation) drug synergism?
flashcards.back: Additive: effect of A+B = effect of A alone + effect of B alone. Supraadditive (Potentiation): effect of A+B > sum of individual effects; occurs when one drug is inactive alone.
A decrease in response to the usual dose of a drug, requiring a dose increase to achieve the same effect.
A specific type of acquired tolerance that develops very rapidly (within minutes). Also called Acute Acquired Tolerance.

flashcards.front: How does Neostigmine inhibit the enzyme Cholinesterase?
flashcards.back: It competes with Acetylcholine for the active site of Cholinesterase (competitive inhibition).

flashcards.front: Why is Aspirin considered an example of irreversible inhibition?
flashcards.back: Because it inhibits the enzyme Cyclooxygenase irreversibly (Irreversible inhibition), preventing prostaglandin synthesis.
The dose that produces 50% of the maximum response in a single animal. Used to compare patient sensitivity and to find equivalent doses.

flashcards.front: What are the four drug targets (RICE)?
flashcards.back: Receptor, Ion channel, Carrier molecule, Enzyme.
It binds to a constitutively active receptor and produces an effect opposite to that of the agonist (negative efficacy).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Tolerance and Tachyphylaxis?
flashcards.back: Tolerance develops slowly; Tachyphylaxis is rapid acute tolerance developing within minutes.
Excess receptors; the maximum response can be achieved without occupying all receptors.
The dose range that produces a therapeutic effect without toxicity.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ED50, TD50, and LD50?
flashcards.back: ED50: effective dose in 50%; TD50: toxic dose in 50%; LD50: lethal dose in 50%.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is defined as a drug that has affinity for the receptor but Efficacy = Zero?
mcqs.explanation: An antagonist binds to the receptor (has affinity) but produces no response (Efficacy = Zero), unlike an agonist which produces a response.

mcqs.q: A drug that requires a smaller amount to produce 50% of the maximum response is described as:
mcqs.explanation: Potency is measured by EC50. A drug that requires a lower concentration (low EC50) is more potent.

mcqs.q: Aspirin inhibits Cyclooxygenase in which manner?
mcqs.explanation: Aspirin inhibits the enzyme Cyclooxygenase irreversibly (Irreversible inhibition); therefore its effect persists until platelets are renewed.

mcqs.q: The Therapeutic Index is calculated by the formula:
mcqs.explanation: TI = TD50 / EC50. It compares the toxic dose to the therapeutic dose. The higher the TI, the safer the drug.

mcqs.q: Histamine lowers blood pressure while Adrenaline raises it. This is an example of:
mcqs.explanation: Physiological antagonism occurs when two drugs act on different receptors and produce opposing effects, such as Histamine and Adrenaline on blood pressure.

mcqs.q: Which of the following drugs is an example of a Partial Agonist?
options: A drug with affinity and partial efficacy
mcqs.explanation: A partial agonist has affinity and some efficacy, but efficacy is incomplete. Alone it acts as an agonist; in the presence of a full agonist it acts as an antagonist.

mcqs.q: Which statement is correct about Competitive Antagonism?
options: It cannot be overcome by increasing the dose
It competes at the same binding site and can be overcome by increasing the agonist dose
It binds to a different site from the agonist
mcqs.explanation: In competitive antagonism, the antagonist competes with the agonist for the same binding site. It can be overcome by increasing the agonist dose and does not alter Emax.

options: Acute tolerance that develops rapidly within minutes
Failure of the drug to bind to the receptor
An increased response to the drug
mcqs.explanation: Tachyphylaxis is a specific type of acquired tolerance that develops very rapidly (within minutes). It is also called Acute Acquired Tolerance.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is correct about Non-competitive antagonism?
options: It reduces Emax and cannot be overcome by increasing the dose
It can be overcome by increasing the agonist dose
It acts on a different receptor
mcqs.explanation: A non-competitive antagonist binds to a site other than the agonist binding site, reducing Emax in a manner that cannot be reversed by increasing the agonist dose.

mcqs.q: On a Dose-Response Curve, which parameter reflects the drug's Efficacy?
mcqs.explanation: Efficacy is reflected by Emax (the maximum response a drug can produce). Potency is measured by EC50.

mcqs.q: Which drug target does Neostigmine represent?
A drug that produces an opposing effect by binding to a constitutively active receptor is:
mcqs.explanation: An inverse agonist has negative efficacy.

high_yield.point: Affinity = ميل الارتباط بالمستقبل؛ Efficacy = القدرة على إحداث استجابة. Antagonist له Affinity وEfficacy = صفر.
Potency تُقاس بـ EC50 (أقل = أقوى)؛ Efficacy تُقاس بـ Emax. مستقلتان عن بعض.
Therapeutic Index = TD50/ED50؛ كلما زاد كان الدواء أأمن.
Partial agonist مع Full agonist يتصرف كـ antagonist (ينافس وينقص الاستجابة القصوى).
Tachyphylaxis = تحمل حاد سريع خلال دقائق (Acute acquired tolerance).
